Name: sparse_attention_kernel_optimization
Title: Hardware-Aware Sparse Attention Kernel Optimization for Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: By designing fused CUDA/Triton kernels that explicitly optimize for sparse attention patterns (e.g., block-sparse, sliding window), we can achieve measurable speedups in transformer inference on commodity GPUs, as measured by end-to-end wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second.
Related Work: Existing work on sparse attention (e.g., Longformer, BigBird) focuses on algorithmic improvements but lacks hardware-level optimizations. Recent efforts like FlashAttention and TritonMoE demonstrate the potential of kernel-level optimizations but do not address sparse attention specifically. This proposal bridges this gap by focusing on hardware-aware kernel design for sparse attention, leveraging profiler-guided optimizations (e.g., memory coalescing, warp stall reduction) to achieve measurable speedups.
Abstract: Sparse attention mechanisms are widely used in transformer models to reduce computational complexity, but their inference efficiency remains limited by suboptimal hardware utilization. This paper presents a systematic approach to optimizing sparse attention kernels for transformer inference on commodity GPUs. We design fused CUDA/Triton kernels that explicitly optimize for common sparse attention patterns (e.g., block-sparse, sliding window), leveraging profiler-guided techniques such as memory coalescing, warp stall reduction, and tensor-core utilization. We evaluate our approach on a range of transformer models (e.g., Longformer, BigBird) using end-to-end wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second as metrics. Our results demonstrate measurable speedups over existing implementations, with detailed profiler traces attributing gains to specific hardware optimizations. Our contributions include open-source kernels, reproducible benchmarks, and guidelines for hardware-aware sparse attention optimization.
Experiments: 1. **Baseline Comparison**: Measure end-to-end latency and tokens-per-second for existing sparse attention implementations (e.g., Longformer, BigBird) on NVIDIA A100.
2. **Kernel Optimization**: Design fused CUDA/Triton kernels for block-sparse and sliding window attention patterns, optimizing for memory coalescing, warp stalls, and tensor-core utilization.
3. **Profiler-Guided Tuning**: Use nvprof/Nsight to identify bottlenecks (e.g., HBM round-trips, bank conflicts) and iteratively refine kernels.
4. **A/B Latency Race**: Compare optimized kernels against baselines under fixed accuracy budgets, reporting p50/p99 latency and throughput under batching.
5. **Energy Efficiency**: Measure energy per token for optimized kernels versus baselines.
6. **Reproducibility**: Archive trace files, commit benchmark harnesses, and pin driver/CUDA versions for reproducibility.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. **Sparse Pattern Variability**: Different sparse attention patterns may require distinct optimizations, limiting generalizability.
2. **Hardware-Specificity**: Optimizations tuned for NVIDIA GPUs may not transfer to other architectures (e.g., AMD MI300X).
3. **Complexity Trade-offs**: Kernel optimizations may introduce additional complexity, requiring careful engineering effort.
4. **Profiling Overhead**: Detailed profiling may add overhead, potentially masking real-world gains.
